# Swiss Longevity Trends, Mortality Compression, and Actuarial Implications for Life Insurance and Pension Systems, 1988-2024

## Abstract

This report tracks Swiss longevity from 1988 to 2024 using period life tables from the Human Mortality Database (HMD). We work with three populations — total, male, female — and the usual life-table toolkit: the survivorship function \(l_x\), the death distribution \(d_x\), the one-year mortality probability \(q_x\), curtate life expectancy at birth \(e_0\), curtate life expectancy at age 65 \(e_{65}\), the total expected lifetime at age 65 expressed as \(e_{65}+65\), and the Lexis Point, which we read here as the modal age at death among older adults. Four findings stand out. Mortality has improved across nearly all ages. The gender gap is real but shrinking. Deaths sit increasingly close to a single advanced-age peak, so the survival curve is more rectangular. The 2020 dip looks like a COVID-era period-table shock rather than a turning point, and 2024 reverts to the pre-existing trajectory. We then carry these demographic results into healthy life expectancy, Swiss pension policy, the life insurance market, and the Actuarial Present Value of Benefits (APVB) for a whole life contract. The headline actuarial point is that one demographic trend produces opposite financial effects: it pushes down the present value of death-contingent benefits when interest rates are positive, while it stretches the expected duration of annuity and pension payments.

## 1. Introduction

Mortality assumptions sit at the center of life-contingent valuation. They drive when cash flows happen, with what probability, and over how long. For death benefits, the exposure is to the probability and timing of death. For annuities and pensions, the exposure flips: the risk is that a population outlives the basis on which premiums, contributions, and reserves were calibrated. That second exposure — systematic longevity risk — is the one we will keep coming back to. The textbook machinery for handling both sits in life-table functions, survival probabilities, discounted cash flows, and actuarial present values (Spedicato, [CHECK REFERENCE DETAILS]; Valdez, 2019).

Switzerland is a useful laboratory for this question. Life expectancy is high, the social security architecture is mature, and the occupational pension sector carries large long-duration obligations. The multi-pillar system has three layers: the first pillar (AHV/AVS) for public old-age and survivors' insurance, the second pillar (BVG/LPP) for mandatory occupational pensions, and a third pillar of private retirement saving. When survival improves in a country like this, the consequences are not just demographic — they touch contribution adequacy, retirement duration, insurer reserving, and the difference between mortality risk and longevity risk.

Our scope is retrospective, not predictive. We do not forecast future mortality. Instead, we read ten benchmark period life tables — 1988, 1992, 1996, 2000, 2004, 2008, 2012, 2016, 2020, 2024 — and ask how the shape of Swiss mortality has changed across them. The benchmark grid covers about 36 years at four-year spacing, which keeps the figures readable while still showing structural movement. We pull in healthy life expectancy from WHO and Swiss policy and market context (AHV 21, the 13th AHV pension, FINMA's life sector statistics) to keep the actuarial reading honest.

The roadmap is straightforward. Section 2 sets up the data, the indicators, and the framework. Section 3 walks through the total, female, and male trends. Section 4 synthesizes the evidence on rectangularization and compression. Section 5 turns to Gompertz and Gompertz-Makeham fitting at old ages. Sections 6 through 8 cover healthy life expectancy, Swiss pension policy, and the life insurance market. Section 9 applies the mortality results to whole life pricing through the APVB. Sections 10 and 11 close with discussion, limitations, and conclusion.

## 2. Data and Actuarial Methodology

### 2.1 Data Source and Sample Design

The empirical material is HMD Swiss period life tables in their standard \(1 \times 1\) form — one-year ages by one-year calendar periods (Human Mortality Database, 2026). HMD provides age-specific mortality in a comparable cross-country format, which is what we need for tracing the shape of the mortality curve year by year. We use period rather than cohort tables throughout. That choice carries a clear interpretive cost: a period table compresses one calendar year's mortality conditions onto a hypothetical cohort, so it tells us about the year, not about the realized lifetime of any real birth cohort.

The one-year age structure matters more than it might look. It is what lets us pin down the Lexis Point inside the adult-old range, watch the \(d_x\) peak slide, and check whether old-age \(q_x\) is genuinely falling or merely shifting. The one-year calendar structure matters in a different way. It lets us isolate 2020 from neighboring years, which is exactly the resolution we need to talk about pandemic-period disruption without smearing it across a four- or five-year window.

Ten benchmark years are pulled from this grid: 1988, 1992, 1996, 2000, 2004, 2008, 2012, 2016, 2020, 2024. The spacing is regular. Using every annual table would give a more textured time series, but the charts would also become noisier and harder to compare visually. So the design favors structural readability over yearly granularity. That is fine for what we want to claim. It would not be fine for measuring annual improvement rates or stochastic mortality forecasting — those would need the full HMD series.

Three population groups are kept separate: total, male, female. The total series is the national aggregate we use for high-level commentary. Splitting by sex is not cosmetic. Swiss female life expectancy remains higher than male life expectancy across the full window, but male improvement appears to run faster from a lower 1988 baseline. That convergence story would disappear if we collapsed the two into a single curve.

Two methodological caveats anchor everything below. First, our life expectancies are period summary measures, not cohort forecasts. Second, with only ten snapshots we describe structure, not year-to-year volatility. Both caveats matter most for 2020 and 2024. A clean read of the pandemic effect would need annual age-specific mortality, excess-mortality decomposition, and cause-of-death data — none of which sit inside our scope.

### 2.2 Life Table Indicators

We use standard life-table notation. Let \(l_x\) denote the survivorship function: the number of lives reaching exact age \(x\) from an initial radix \(l_0\), commonly 100,000 in HMD tables. Normalized as \(l_x/l_0\), it reads as a survival probability from birth to age \(x\). The \(l_x\) curve is the headline object for rectangularization, because rectangularization is essentially a statement about its shape.

The death distribution \(d_x\) counts deaths between exact ages \(x\) and \(x+1\):

$$
d_x = l_x - l_{x+1}.
$$

This is a different object from \(q_x\). \(d_x\) tells you where deaths happen in the life-table cohort; a peak shifting rightward says the modal age at death is moving later, and a narrower peak says deaths are concentrating into a tighter age band — the textbook fingerprint of compression.

The one-year mortality probability conditional on survival to age \(x\) is:

$$
q_x = \frac{d_x}{l_x}.
$$

A low \(q_{40}\) means a 40-year-old is unlikely to die before 41. It does not by itself say much about how many deaths cluster around age 40 in the original cohort — \(d_x\) carries that information. We will therefore treat \(l_x\), \(d_x\), and \(q_x\) as three lenses on the same table, not as substitutes.

Curtate life expectancy at age \(x\) is the expected number of complete future years lived:

$$
e_x = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{l_{x+k}}{l_x}.
$$

Equivalently, this is the sum of future survival probabilities from \(x\), since \(l_{x+k}/l_x\) is the life-table probability that a life aged \(x\) survives at least \(k\) complete years (Valdez, 2019). Two values do most of the work: \(e_0\) and \(e_{65}\). We also report \(e_{65}+65\), which simply re-expresses the conditional expectation at 65 as an expected total age at death. The transformation is small but it matters for chart reading: \(e_0\), \(e_{65}+65\), and the Lexis Point now sit on the same age scale, so the gap between them is interpretable directly.

A standard mistake worth flagging: a rise in \(e_{65}\) is not the same thing as a rise in \(e_0\) of the same magnitude. \(e_{65}\) is conditional on having already reached 65, so its movement reflects only old-age mortality improvement. \(e_0\) absorbs improvements at every age.

We treat the Lexis Point as the modal age at death within the adult-old portion of the table — operationally, the age at which \(d_x\) is largest in that region. Pitacco and Carmeci (2019) use exactly this kind of marker, together with \(e_0\) and \(e_{65}+65\), to talk about longevity expansion and the concentration of deaths. We borrow that framing. We do not borrow any claim that the Lexis Point is a biological cap on lifespan; it is a descriptive marker that can shift if the table shifts.

### 2.3 Analytical Framework

Four ideas drive the analysis: mortality improvement, rectangularization, mortality compression, and actuarial liability translation. Improvement is the simplest: \(q_x\) falls at many ages, \(l_x\) stays higher into later ages, \(e_0\) or \(e_{65}\) rises, and the \(d_x\) peak migrates rightward. These four signals usually move together, but they answer different questions and we will keep them separate when they disagree.

Rectangularization is a shape claim about \(l_x/l_0\). A more rectangular curve stays near one through childhood, working age, and early retirement, then drops sharply at older ages. It signals that survival has become less dispersed across the life course. It does not say early deaths have disappeared, and it certainly does not impose a fixed lifespan ceiling.

Mortality compression is a distributional claim about \(d_x\). When the main peak moves rightward and tightens, ages at death are clustering into a narrower old-age window — the framing used by Pitacco and Carmeci (2019), where \(e_0\), \(e_{65}+65\), and the Lexis Point are read jointly to separate general survival gain from old-age concentration.

The gender dimension sits inside this framework as well. Female survival is typically higher in developed-country tables, but male catch-up from a lower baseline can be faster. So we run total, female, and male separately before doing any convergence reading.

The last piece is liability translation. Lower mortality before and after retirement raises the number of survivors collecting pension or annuity payments. That makes longevity assumptions central to AHV/AVS, BVG/LPP, and annuity-style products. The opposite holds for a fixed-benefit whole life contract paid at the end of the year of death: improved survival pushes the expected claim later, and at a positive technical rate this lowers the APVB. So one demographic trend, two opposite financial signs — that contrast is the spine of the report.

## 3. Historical Longevity Trends in Switzerland

### 3.1 Overall Population Trend

We start with the national aggregate because it captures the policy-relevant baseline. The total series blends the historically higher female survival profile with the faster male improvement curve, so it sits between the two. The chart below plots three summary indicators for the total Swiss population across the benchmark grid: \(e_0\), \(e_{65}+65\), and the Lexis Point. Direction-wise, the picture is unambiguous — Swiss longevity moves up over 1988-2024. We do not have the underlying total-population benchmark table in front of us; the exact values should be cross-checked against the HMD calculation file before final submission [NEED EMPIRICAL VALUE].

![Total population longevity indicators, Switzerland, 1988-2024. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image16]

The rise in \(e_0\) is the headline number. Demographically, it says average period-table lifetime is longer. Actuarially, it does not say much on its own, because \(e_0\) is sensitive to deaths at every age — including childhood — and that is not where pension liabilities live. The more useful indicator for retirement-income work is \(e_{65}+65\). Its upward path tells us that improvement is not bottled up in young-age mortality; it reaches the over-65 cohort too. That is what matters for AHV/AVS, BVG/LPP, and annuity portfolios, because the cost of those liabilities is driven by survival after retirement.

The Lexis Point adds the distributional angle. In the total-population chart the modal age at death migrates upward and settles in the 90-91 region in the later years, lining up with what the original calculations showed. We are not claiming a biological ceiling. We are saying the densest mortality mass in the period table has moved later. When \(e_0\), \(e_{65}+65\), and the Lexis Point start to crowd toward each other on the same age axis, that is the visual signature of compression: fewer deaths far below the modal range, more deaths clustered at advanced ages.

The total-population survival curve \(l_x/l_0\) backs the rectangularization reading developed later. Across the benchmark grid the survival profile stays high through childhood, adulthood, and early retirement before dropping at older ages. More of the period cohort therefore reaches retirement-age exposures. For annuity and pension liabilities — where headcount at each payment age is the cost driver — that is the consequential change.

The death distribution \(d_x\) gives the complementary view. A rightward, more concentrated peak does not just say "people live longer on average"; it says the timing of death is becoming more predictable around the modal range. For pension planners that is a scheduling fact: large retiree cohorts will arrive at the main mortality ages in a tighter window. For insurers it shapes claim runoff patterns and the timing of reserve releases.

Behind both is the conditional risk story told by \(q_x\). Later benchmark years show lower mortality probabilities across many adult and younger-old ages, with the expected steep rise still in place at advanced ages. So the visual movement in \(l_x\) and \(d_x\) is not artefactual; it is being produced by genuinely lower conditional probabilities at ages where more people now survive. The limit is that \(q_x\) alone cannot tell us *why* mortality improved — by cause, by socioeconomic group, by canton, by cohort. Our reading stays at the period-table level.

2020 needs its own treatment. Several panels show a visible interruption or flattening that lines up with COVID timing. The life-table charts do not identify cause of death, so we cannot push beyond that. The 2024 point looks like recovery toward the pre-existing trend, but a sharper pandemic read would need annual age-specific mortality, excess-mortality measures, and cause data. For our purposes the 2020 dip is best read as a period-table shock layered onto a longer improvement trend.

### 3.2 Female Longevity Trend

The female series is interesting precisely because it starts from an already high baseline — this is improvement on top of an advanced survival profile, not a transition from low to high mortality. The numbers from the author calculations: female \(e_0\) rises from 80.23 years in 1988 to 84.57 in 2020 and 85.40 in 2024. The old-age indicator \(e_{65}+65\) moves from 83.97 in 1988 to 86.68 in 2020 and 87.46 in 2024. The Lexis Point shifts from 86 in 1988 to 91 by 2008, and stays at 91 through 2024.

![Female longevity indicators, Switzerland, 1988-2024. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image1]

The \(e_0\) movement says period-table average lifetime is longer for Swiss women across the window. Because the 1988 baseline was already high, the right framing is continued improvement at the frontier, not catch-up. The \(e_{65}+65\) climb confirms that the gains are not bottled up at younger ages — women who reach 65 are also living longer beyond 65. That directly extends annuity duration, pension payment periods, and the reserve needed to back them.

The Lexis Point story is about concentration. The shift from 86 to around 91 says the modal age at death among older women moved later. The fact that the Lexis Point then sits stably at 91 across the recent years suggests deaths are accumulating tightly around advanced ages. Read this as a period mode, not a biological endpoint. Different years, different cohorts, different conditions can shift it.

Visually the female \(l_x/l_0\) curve is the strongest rectangularization case in the report. Survival stays high across the early and middle ages and then falls late. Most of the female period cohort reaches retirement. For pension and annuity liabilities — especially products without automatic mortality-improvement adjustments — that is straightforwardly a higher exposure base.

The female \(d_x\) chart reinforces the compression reading. In the earlier years deaths are spread more widely across old ages; in the later years the main peak shifts rightward and tightens around the modal range. So female mortality is not only delayed on average; it is concentrating in time. Visual concentration in \(d_x\) is not a formal compression test, of course. A proper test would compute dispersion above the modal age or report standard-deviation-above-mode statistics. We have not done that here.

The female \(q_x\) profile carries the age-specific mechanism. Later years show lower conditional mortality across much of the younger-old range, while very advanced ages still see a steep rise. So improvement delays deaths into later ages without removing the underlying risk. For annuity and pension valuation this matters because women dominate the long-payment tail. The interpretive limit is the obvious one: this is the period-table picture, not a guarantee that premature mortality has vanished. The safer claim is that younger-age mortality is less visible relative to old-age mortality in these particular profiles.

### 3.3 Male Longevity Trend

If the female series is improvement at the frontier, the male series is catch-up — and it is the bigger story numerically. Male \(e_0\) climbs from 73.40 years in 1988 to 80.50 in 2020 and 81.92 in 2024. That is an 8.52-year gain across 36 years. Over the same window \(e_{65}+65\) rises from 79.80 to 84.94. The male Lexis Point moves from 83 in 1988 to 90 in 2024 — a seven-year shift in the modal age at death.

![Male longevity indicators, Switzerland, 1988-2024. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image8]

For context, in 1988 the male \(e_0\) sat nearly seven years below the female value reported above. By 2024 that gap has closed to about 3.48 years. Male and female mortality schedules have not become identical — the gap is still real — but the male table has moved faster from a worse starting point. The \(e_{65}+65\) rise also says the improvement is not just at younger and middle ages; it is showing up after retirement too. For pension systems that means male retirees increasingly drive payment-duration risk as well.

The Lexis Point shift from 83 to 90 deserves attention. In 1988 the distance between male \(e_0\) and the male modal age at death was large — a lot of male mortality happened before the modal old-age range. By 2024 the modal age has migrated into the advanced range and is close to the female value. So male death timing is concentrating later. Like before, we frame this as a descriptive period-table observation rather than a structural claim.

The male survival curve shows the same story in shape terms. The 1988 curve falls visibly before old age. The 2024 curve sits higher through working ages and early retirement before declining. Demographically: lower male mortality before the main old-age decline. Actuarially: more male lives stay exposed to retirement-age payments, and death benefits are expected later than under the older basis.

![Male survival function, Switzerland, selected benchmark years. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image9]

The \(d_x\) panels track the same shift. Earlier male profiles look wider and more left-loaded; later ones cluster around old ages. The \(q_x\) chart gives the mechanism behind the numbers. Young-adult and middle-age mortality probabilities are lower across the sample, and if the chart shows a smaller young-adult elevation in later years, that could be the traditional male "accident hump" softening. We need to stop short of attribution — the chart does not identify workplace safety, behavior, smoking, alcohol, or mental health as causes. The defensible reading is that the male profiles show lower conditional mortality through parts of young and middle adulthood.

Pricing implications split by product. For male annuities and pensions, improved survival lengthens expected payment duration and removes the earlier-mortality offset that used to ease liability cost. For whole life insurance, the same improvement defers the expected death benefit and lowers APVB under a positive rate. Male mortality remains higher than female mortality at many ages, so male APVB can still sit above female APVB even while both fall over the period.

### 3.4 Gender Comparison and Convergence

Two findings carry this subsection. Females stay ahead in life expectancy across the entire window. Males improve faster, so the gap narrows. The arithmetic: female \(e_0\) rises by about 5.17 years from 1988 to 2024; male \(e_0\) rises by about 8.52 years. The male series closes part of its historical survival deficit without fully converging. In 2024 the residual \(e_0\) gap is about 3.48 years.

The comparison chart focuses on distribution, not average. It overlays the male and female \(d_x\) profiles, which lets us see where deaths cluster rather than only how long the average period lifetime is. In 1988 the modal ages at death are 86 (female) and 83 (male). By 2024 they are 91 and 90. The death-distribution peaks have converged around the advanced-age region.

![Comparison of male and female death distributions, Switzerland. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image12]

Demographically this is convergence in the *location* of the modal death age, not convergence in the underlying schedule. Female advantage at the average level persists. Actuarially the implication is more subtle: gender-specific mortality assumptions still matter, but the source of the difference is changing. Earlier tables encoded larger gaps in premature and middle-age mortality. Later tables show more similarity at the advanced-age concentration of deaths. A pricing model that only looks at \(e_0\) would miss this distributional movement entirely, because \(e_0\) does not tell you whether deaths are dispersed or concentrated.

Two populations can share a Lexis Point and still carry different \(q_x\) schedules, different retirement-age survival probabilities, and different expected present values for insurance benefits. So the right operational conclusion is balanced. Female longevity remains higher. Male improvement is faster. The average gap narrows but does not vanish. Modal ages at death converge near 90-91. Sex-specific analysis stays in the toolkit wherever legally and institutionally permitted, and mortality assumptions need updating as male catch-up keeps reshaping claim timing and retirement payment patterns.

## 4. Mortality Compression and Survival Curve Rectangularization

Two related ideas pull the Swiss evidence together: rectangularization of the survival curve, and mortality compression. They are not the same claim. Rectangularization is about the shape of \(l_x/l_0\) — a curve is more rectangular when it sits close to one through childhood, working ages, and early retirement, and then falls steeply at advanced ages. Compression is about the distribution of ages at death — it shows up when deaths concentrate inside a tighter old-age window, typically as a rightward, narrower \(d_x\) peak. Across the Swiss period tables, both fingerprints appear more strongly in the later years than in 1988.

The clearest visual is the survival-function panel. It plots \(l_x/l_0\), the share of the period cohort surviving from birth to each exact age. Later benchmark years sit higher across most of the age range and then drop more sharply at older ages than the earlier profiles. Demographically: more of the period cohort reaches old age. Actuarially: more lives remain in force for products whose liabilities depend on survival into retirement — pensions, annuities, and longevity-linked guarantees.

![Survival function \(l_x/l_0\), Switzerland, selected years. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image2]

Rectangularization needs careful handling. It says deaths are less spread across childhood, young adulthood, and middle age relative to the earlier period. It does not say survival has become certain until old age. Young-age deaths still happen, and these are period life tables — they do not follow a real cohort through calendar time. The safer reading: the selected Swiss period tables show higher survival across many ages and a later mortality concentration, not the disappearance of mortality risk.

The death distribution \(d_x\) complements the survival curve by showing where deaths land inside the cohort. If \(l_x\) holds higher into later ages, the deaths that previously spread across a wider range get pushed toward older ages. In the figures the main \(d_x\) peak migrates rightward and tightens. That is the compression signature: ages at death are less dispersed and more centered on advanced ages. The rightward shift is also what drives the rising Lexis Point in the indicator charts.

![Death distribution \(d_x\), Switzerland, selected years. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image3]

Zooming in helps separate two channels. The magnified early-life and segmented panels show infant and young-age deaths shrinking to a small fraction of total deaths in the later years. That movement is what lifts \(e_0\), because life expectancy at birth is acutely sensitive to deaths at very young ages. But the old-age portion of the chart carries equal weight. A larger and later old-age death peak says that people who survive early and middle ages now reach the main retirement-age mortality range in greater numbers. That second channel is the one annuity duration and pension exposure actually care about.

![Early-life and older-age death distribution panels. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image4]

![Magnified early-life death distribution. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image5]

The mortality probability curve \(q_x\) closes the loop. It tells us the probability of dying within one year given survival to age \(x\). The Swiss profiles follow the standard human pattern: elevated at age zero, very low through childhood and young adulthood, steeply rising at older ages. Later-year curves sit lower across many adult and old-age ranges. That downward movement is the engine — it keeps \(l_x\) higher, pushes \(d_x\) rightward, lifts \(e_0\), and raises \(e_{65}+65\).

![Mortality probability \(q_x\), Switzerland, selected years. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image6]

Read together, the Lexis Point, \(e_0\), and \(e_{65}+65\) describe one structure from three angles. \(e_0\) rises: the average period-table lifetime is longer. \(e_{65}+65\) rises: survival improvement reaches the over-65 cohort. The Lexis Point rises: the modal age at death migrates to later ages. When all three move up and the gap between \(e_0\) and the old-age indicators narrows, the joint picture is survival improvement, death postponement, and concentration — three signals, one phenomenon.

The actuarial bite lands hardest on retirement-income liabilities. More people reach 65, and those who do live longer beyond 65. Pensions and annuities therefore face longer expected payment durations. Compression sharpens the timing problem because payments now cluster across a longer and more predictable old-age window rather than being partly absorbed by earlier deaths. Mortality risk has not disappeared — it has changed location. For annuity and pension providers the risk concentrates in old-age survival and systematic improvement. For whole life insurance the same delay in death defers fixed benefit payments and tends to lower APVB under positive discounting.

One caveat carries the whole section. Everything above rests on visual evidence from a sparse benchmark grid. The charts are internally coherent, but they are not a formal decomposition of mortality improvement by age, cause, cohort, or sex. A proper compression test would compute dispersion measures around the modal age. A proper rectangularization test would specify a survival-curve shape metric. We do neither here. So the conclusion stays cautious: the Swiss period tables show evidence consistent with rectangularization and compression.

## 5. Gompertz and Gompertz-Makeham Mortality Fitting

The old-age portion of \(q_x\) can also be read through a parametric lens. The Gompertz model is the classical workhorse for adult and old-age mortality: the force of mortality rises exponentially with age,

$$
\mu_x = Bc^x, \quad B > 0,\ c > 1.
$$

It captures the empirical regularity that adult mortality looks approximately linear on a log-mortality scale. That makes it a clean summary tool for senescent mortality. It is not designed for young ages, where accidents, external causes, and infectious events sit on top of any age-driven baseline.

Gompertz-Makeham adds an age-independent piece:

$$
\mu_x = A + Bc^x.
$$

The Makeham term \(A\) is the background-mortality component — a floor that does not depend on age. The distinction matters here because the Swiss profiles in later years show very low young and middle-age mortality next to a still-steep old-age rise, which is the shape Gompertz-Makeham was built to describe.

The 2024 fitting chart overlays Swiss empirical mortality probabilities with the Gompertz and Gompertz-Makeham fitted curves on a log scale. The role of this chart is exploratory, not confirmatory. Visually, the old-age portion of Swiss mortality in 2024 lines up with an exponential acceleration over the fitted age range, which matches the standard demographic regularity that mortality compounds rapidly at older ages.

![Gompertz and Gompertz-Makeham fit to 2024 mortality probabilities. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image7]

What this chart can support: the reading that old-age Swiss mortality in 2024 remains strongly age-dependent, and the linkage between the earlier \(q_x\) evidence and a familiar parametric form. Lower mortality at adult and younger-old ages pushes survival to later ages; the exponential old-age rise then produces a steep final decline in \(l_x\). That is rectangularization with a parametric description attached.

What the chart cannot support without a real fit table: claims about whether Switzerland has reached a "pure" biological mortality structure, whether background mortality has effectively vanished, or whether the Makeham term \(A\) is statistically distinguishable from zero. Those would need fitted parameter estimates, standard errors, residual diagnostics, the exact age range used in estimation, and a comparison of model fit. None of that sits in the chart. So we treat Gompertz and Gompertz-Makeham here as descriptive devices, not as proof of the underlying mortality mechanism.

In the wider structure of the report this section plays a supporting role. It gives a parametric language for the old-age \(q_x\) story but does not displace the life-table evidence from \(l_x\), \(d_x\), \(q_x\), \(e_0\), \(e_{65}+65\), and the Lexis Point. The empirical bottom line stays life-table based: Swiss mortality improvement has shifted survival and deaths toward advanced ages, and mortality risk still accelerates sharply at older ages.

## 6. Healthy Life Expectancy versus Total Life Expectancy

Total Life Expectancy (TLE), Healthy Life Expectancy (HLE), and Health-Adjusted Life Expectancy (HALE) describe overlapping but distinct things. TLE measures expected years lived from birth or from some specified age. It is close in spirit to the period-table life expectancy measures used above, although our HMD numbers are curtate. HLE and HALE adjust expected years for health status. WHO defines HALE at birth as the average number of years a person can expect to live in full health, after subtracting years lived in less than full health due to disease or injury (World Health Organization, n.d.).

That distinction is not cosmetic. The actuarial meaning of longer survival depends on whether the extra years are mostly healthy, mostly disabled, or medically intensive. Two cases bracket the policy debate. If TLE and HALE rise by similar amounts, the data lean toward morbidity compression — longer lives push unhealthy years out alongside healthy ones. If TLE outruns HALE, the data lean toward morbidity expansion — total years rise, but some of the additional years are lived with morbidity, disability, or functional limitation. WHO figures alone do not pick a winner between these stories, but they let us compare total survival to healthy survival in a consistent way.

The Swiss WHO numbers point to high total life expectancy and high healthy life expectancy. For the total population, WHO TLE at birth rose from about 79.7 years in 2000 to about 83.3 years in 2021. Over the same window HALE rose from about 68.4 years to about 71.1 years. The implied TLE-HALE gap therefore widened from roughly 11.4 to roughly 12.2 years. The sex-specific values reinforce the need for careful reading: in 2021, female TLE was about 85.1 years against female HALE of 71.3, while male TLE was about 81.5 years against male HALE of 70.9 (World Health Organization, n.d.).

| ![WHO life expectancy chart for Switzerland. Source: WHO country data.][image17] | ![WHO sex-specific life expectancy chart for Switzerland. Source: WHO country data.][image18] |
| :-- | :-- |

The Swiss gap should not be over-interpreted. It says there is space between total years lived and years expected in full health. It does not say every extra year is unhealthy. That said, the gap is actuarially load-bearing. A larger period outside full health may raise exposure to health insurance claims, disability spending, long-term care needs, informal caregiving, and public health costs. For pensions the implication is subtler: longer life extends benefit duration, but lower HALE relative to TLE can also constrain the ability or willingness of older workers to remain employed. So longevity analysis and retirement-age policy are linked through health, not just through survival.

Pitacco and Carmeci (2019) lay out the theoretical alternatives — morbidity compression, equilibrium, and expansion. Applied to Switzerland, the joint HMD and WHO evidence supports a cautious framing. HMD shows longer survival and compressed mortality; WHO shows HALE running materially below TLE. Together these point to a need to keep total and healthy longevity separate. They do not certify which morbidity regime Switzerland is in without additional morbidity, disability, long-term care, or healthcare utilization data.

![WHO healthy life expectancy comparison for Switzerland. Source: WHO country data.][image19]

The actuarial takeaway extends beyond pension and life cash flows. Pension and annuity providers face longer expected payment durations as survival improves. Health insurers, long-term care providers, households, and public budgets may face higher late-life care exposure if some of the additional life years carry chronic illness or functional limitation. We are not pricing long-term care insurance here and we are not modeling healthcare costs. The TLE-HALE comparison still earns its place in the report because it shows why an honest actuarial reading of longevity should separate survival extension from healthy-survival extension.

A few data caveats. WHO TLE and HALE are reported here for 2000-2021, while our HMD analysis extends to 2024. The WHO figures are not curtate HMD life-table values, and we do not splice them into the HMD \(e_0\) series. HALE also depends on morbidity weights, health-state assumptions, and international comparability methods — so the number carries methodological baggage that \(e_0\) does not. For that reason we use WHO HALE as contextual evidence around the HMD work, not as a direct input into the APVB calculation in Section 9.

## 7. Swiss Pension and Policy Implications

Switzerland's pension architecture is directly exposed to the longevity trends documented in the HMD tables. The first pillar, AHV/AVS, provides public old-age and survivors' insurance. The second pillar, BVG/LPP, is the occupational pension system, designed to top up the state pillar with employment-linked retirement income (Swiss Confederation, n.d.-a). The third pillar covers private pension provision and voluntary retirement saving (Swiss Confederation, n.d.-b). The actuarial point about this structure is simple. Longevity improvement reaches both public and occupational schemes: more people survive to retirement, and those who do collect benefits for longer.

The first-pillar pressure point sits in the ratio between contributors and beneficiaries. A longer retirement period stretches benefit payments per retiree, unless retirement age, contributions, benefits, or financing rules move. The HMD evidence does not measure AHV cash flows directly, but it pins down the demographic mechanism that drives first-pillar sensitivity to longevity. \(e_{65}+65\) rises. The Lexis Point migrates later. The survival curve stays high into old age. That is the textbook setup for longer expected duration of retirement payments.

In the second pillar the channel is different. Longevity feeds through reserves, annuity conversion factors, technical interest assumptions, and asset-liability management. If retirees live longer than the basis used at the point of conversion, the same accumulated pension capital has to finance a longer expected payment stream. That is the occupational-pension flavor of systematic longevity risk. It is slow-moving, population-wide, and hard to diversify inside a single national pool.

AHV 21 sits in that context as a parameter reform in a longer-survival environment. The Federal Social Insurance Office reports that AHV 21 entered into force on January 1, 2024. The reform introduces the term "reference age" and sets a common reference age of 65 for men and women, while gradually raising the women's reference age from 64 to 65. The staged transition runs 64 years and 3 months in 2025, 64 years and 6 months in 2026, 64 years and 9 months in 2027, and 65 from 2028 (Federal Social Insurance Office, 2025).

We do not present AHV 21 as a mechanical consequence of the life-table charts. The safer reading is that the same demographic forces visible in HMD form part of the broader environment in which pension reforms get designed and debated. Longer old-age survival raises the importance of contribution duration, flexible retirement timing, benefit adequacy, and financing sustainability. The AHV 21 transition also illustrates why gender-specific longevity work still matters: female life expectancy has historically been higher, while the legal reference age is moving toward alignment between men and women.

The 13th AHV pension adds a second policy thread. The Federal Social Insurance Office states that the 13th AHV pension will be paid for the first time in December 2026, and that on the referenced 2026 page financing was still under parliamentary discussion. The page also confirms that the 13th pension is paid once per year in December as a supplement to the December pension for old-age pension recipients (Federal Social Insurance Office, 2026). Longevity is not the cause of this measure, but it shapes its fiscal footprint: a recurring pension enhancement applies over a longer expected payment horizon when more people survive longer after retirement.

The combined picture is that Swiss pension policy has to be read alongside mortality improvement, not separately from it. Rectangularization and compression imply that a larger share of the population reaches the ages where pensions are paid. Rising \(e_{65}+65\) tells us retirement duration is not static. At the same time the HLE/TLE gap pushes back against any reading that takes TLE as the only relevant longevity number — extended working lives and later retirement depend on healthy life expectancy, labor-market capacity, and late-life care needs as well. We therefore frame Swiss pension implications as a risk-management problem, not as a one-instrument policy argument.

## 8. Swiss Life Insurance Market Context

The Swiss life insurance sector is the institutional setting for the report's actuarial reading. The market matters here because the demographic changes documented above are not confined to population statistics. They feed into premium adequacy, reserving, solvency assessment, and the expected timing of benefits in long-duration life and pension-related products. We use Gross Written Premiums (GWP) as the flow measure of annual business volume and technical liabilities and actuarial reserves as the balance-sheet measure of obligations already accumulated.

FINMA's 2024 insurance market report puts total Swiss insurance GWP at CHF 150.0 billion in 2024, up 6.7% from 2023. Inside that, the life insurance sector wrote CHF 23.666 billion in GWP, down 8.4% from the previous year. FINMA attributes the life-sector decline in part to reinsurance accepted and to a special effect tied to the conversion of a foreign branch into a foreign subsidiary. Life-insurer annual profits rose 21.7% to CHF 1.587 billion (FINMA, 2025). These two numbers deserve separate reading: premium volume measures business written during the year, while profit reflects underwriting, investment, reserving, and other operating effects.

The balance-sheet side connects more directly to longevity risk than annual GWP. FINMA reports Swiss life insurers held a balance-sheet total of CHF 290.236 billion, investments of CHF 267.558 billion, and technical liabilities of CHF 252.269 billion in 2024. For the insurance market as a whole, technical liabilities reach CHF 512.036 billion, but the life-insurer figure is what matters for this section. The Swiss Solvency Test (SST) ratio for life insurers was 227% in 2024, against 246% for the total insurance market (FINMA, 2025). Those ratios indicate a substantial regulatory capital buffer. They do not remove the need for credible mortality assumptions — long-duration liabilities stay sensitive to persistent changes in survival, interest rates, and product guarantees.

The composition of life premiums confirms how tightly Swiss life insurance is wired to occupational pensions. FINMA puts group life occupational pension schemes at CHF 14.375 billion of life GWP in 2024, or 60.7% of the life total. Gross actuarial reserves for life insurers stand at CHF 216.372 billion, with group life occupational pension schemes representing 57.8% of that reserve base (FINMA, 2025). That composition is actuarially load-bearing. Group life business sits close to the Swiss second pillar (BVG/LPP), so mortality improvement does not just touch individual protection products — it works through pension-related guarantees, conversion-rate assumptions, and the duration of retirement benefits.

Market concentration is the next visible feature in FINMA's direct Swiss business table. The six largest life insurers account for 85.5% of direct Swiss life premium volume in 2024. Swiss Life leads at 41.0%, followed by Helvetia Leben at 12.4%, Baloise Leben at 10.2%, AXA Leben at 9.0%, Allianz Suisse Leben at 7.2%, and Zurich Leben at 5.7% (FINMA, 2025). The market is concentrated. That is a defensible description; it is not a claim about profitability, pricing power, or systemic importance for each firm — those would need additional financial and supervisory evidence.

The market context reinforces why mortality risk and longevity risk need separating. Death benefits are exposed to the probability and timing of death. Annuities, pension guarantees, and retirement-income products are exposed to survival after retirement. A reduction in mortality can both delay death claims and lower the present value of a fixed whole life death benefit under positive discounting, while at the same time extending the expected duration of annuity and pension payments. Because the Swiss life sector carries a substantial reserve base and a strong occupational-pension component, mortality assumptions matter for product pricing and for solvency management.

## 9. Pricing Evolution of Whole Life Insurance, 1988-2024

### 9.1 Product Definition and Assumptions

This section pushes the mortality results through a simple whole life valuation. The contract is issued at age 35 and pays a fixed death benefit of EUR 400,000 at the end of the year of death. The technical interest rate is fixed at 2.5% per annum. We repeat the calculation across the same ten HMD benchmark years used above (1988 to 2024), and separately for the male, female, and total Swiss period life tables.

The product is death-contingent, not survival-contingent. If the contract stays in force until death, the benefit is paid eventually. What changes is when. A mortality table with higher early and middle-age death probabilities loads more probability mass onto earlier claim years. A table with improved survival shifts mass to later claim years. With a positive technical rate, later expected payment lowers present value — that is the whole game.

The valuation is deliberately stripped down. It is deterministic and life-table based. It leaves out acquisition and maintenance expenses, profit loading, surrender values, lapses, underwriting classes, tax treatment, capital costs, currency conversion, and stochastic interest rates. The benefit is denominated in EUR while the mortality basis is Swiss. So we read these APVB numbers as a mortality-driven actuarial comparison across years, not as market premiums. If the final submission uses any of this as pricing evidence, the currency, expense, and discount-rate assumptions need explicit justification.

### 9.2 APVB Formula

Let \(x=35\) be the entry age, \(B\) the sum assured, \(i=0.025\) the technical interest rate, and \(v=(1+i)^{-1}\) the annual discount factor. Let \({}_kp_x\) denote the probability that a life aged \(x\) survives \(k\) years, and let \(q_{x+k}\) denote the one-year probability of death between ages \(x+k\) and \(x+k+1\). In life-table notation, \({}_kp_x\) is just \(l_{x+k}/l_x\), subject to the age range of the table. For a benefit paid at the end of the year of death, the Actuarial Present Value of Benefits (APVB) is:

$$
APVB = B \times \sum_{k=0}^{\omega-x-1} v^{k+1} \cdot {}_kp_x \cdot q_{x+k}.
$$

The sum runs over every policy year in which death can occur. The term \({}_kp_x q_{x+k}\) is the probability that the insured survives to the start of policy year \(k+1\) and then dies during that year. The factor \(v^{k+1}\) discounts the death benefit from the end of the year of death back to issue. This is the standard life-contingent present-value construction for whole life (Spedicato, [CHECK REFERENCE DETAILS]). The asymmetry with annuities sits right inside this expression: a later death increases the discount period for a death benefit, but it lengthens the payment stream for an annuity.

### 9.3 Empirical Pricing Trend

![APVB of a EUR 400,000 whole life insurance contract issued at age 35, Switzerland, 1988-2024. Source: author calculations from HMD period life tables.][image20]

The APVB chart shows the estimated present value of the EUR 400,000 whole life death benefit under each benchmark mortality table. The visual pattern slopes downward across all three series — total, male, female — from 1988 to 2024. That direction lines up with the mortality evidence built in Sections 3 and 4. Improved survival shifts deaths to older ages, so the expected claim payment is deferred. At a 2.5% technical rate, deferral compounds, and the present value of the death-contingent benefit falls.

The sex-specific ordering also makes actuarial sense. Male APVB sits above female APVB because the male mortality schedule loads more probability onto earlier death. Earlier expected death means a shorter discount window and a higher present value for the same fixed benefit. Female APVB sits lower because higher survival probabilities push claim probability into later years. The total population series sits between the two — it inherits the combined mortality experience.

2020 interrupts the long-run downward trend. That is what we would expect from a pandemic-period mortality shock in a period table. If age-specific mortality rises temporarily, the probability of earlier death goes up and the expected payment date moves forward. Under the APVB formula that lifts the present value of the fixed death benefit. The 2024 point looks like recovery toward the longer-run trajectory. I read this as a period-table recovery signal, not as evidence that all pandemic mortality effects have faded.

The old draft reports the APVB falling by more than 15% across the full period. The direction is supported both by the chart and by the actuarial mechanism, but the exact percentage needs to be checked against the underlying spreadsheet titled "APVB_WholeLife_Switzerland_Results" before final submission: [CHECK APVB RESULT TABLE]. Without that calculation table in hand, the report should not present the percentage as an audited result.

### 9.4 Actuarial Interpretation

The result lines up the asymmetry that frames this whole report. Mortality improvement lowers the APVB of a fixed whole life death benefit at a positive discount rate, because the expected claim is delayed. The same mortality improvement raises annuity and pension liabilities, because payments stretch over a longer expected period. One demographic trend, opposite financial signs — depending on whether the product pays on death or pays while the insured is alive.

That asymmetry has practical weight in a market like Switzerland, where life insurers run both protection business and pension-related business. For whole life, a static mortality basis pulled from an earlier high-mortality year can overstate the expected present value of a fixed death benefit once mortality has improved materially. For annuities and pensions the opposite trap dominates: an outdated mortality basis understates survival duration and reserves. So the insurer needs product-specific mortality monitoring, not a single global verdict that improvement is "good" or "bad" for the book.

Dynamic mortality tables also matter because the trend is not smooth. The 2020 interruption shows how a short-run mortality shock can move APVB in the opposite direction from the longer-run improvement trend. If pricing or reserving leans on one recent period table, it risks confusing a temporary disruption with a structural shift. A more robust setup compares multiple years, monitors age-specific \(q_x\), and tests sensitivity to alternative improvement assumptions.

## 10. Discussion

Pulled together, the HMD analysis says Switzerland improved its mortality steadily from 1988 to 2024. The improvement shows up in rising life expectancy measures, a rightward Lexis Point, a higher \(l_x\) profile, and lower or later-rising \(q_x\) probabilities. These indicators do not measure the same object, but they agree. More people survive to old age, deaths concentrate at advanced ages, and the average and modal timing of death both move later.

The gender results sharpen that picture. Female longevity stays higher across the window, which matches the female advantage visible in the life expectancy and survival-curve profiles. Male improvement runs faster from a lower 1988 baseline, so the gap narrows and modal ages at death move closer together — without erasing sex-specific differences. For actuarial work that means watching convergence carefully without collapsing male and female mortality bases until the residual differences look immaterial for the product being valued.

The \(l_x\), \(d_x\), and \(q_x\) synthesis supports a joint reading of rectangularization and compression. The survival function holds nearer to its initial level for longer — fewer deaths at young and middle ages. The death distribution shifts rightward and concentrates around advanced ages. Mortality probabilities locate the risk more squarely at older ages rather than spreading it across the adult life course. The actuarial translation is a cash-flow one: longer expected duration for pensions and annuities, deferred fixed death benefit payments for whole life insurance.

The healthy-life-expectancy material is the part that prevents us from reading longevity as a single positive shift. The WHO TLE and HALE evidence places Switzerland in a high-TLE, high-HALE bracket, with a material and slowly widening gap between total years and full-health years (World Health Organization, n.d.). That gap reaches into different parts of the financial system. Pensions are sensitive to how long benefits are paid. Health insurance and long-term care are sensitive to the health status of those years. Households are sensitive to the affordability and quality of late-life care. We therefore treat longevity as a joint problem of survival, morbidity, and benefit design, not as a single demographic dial.

The Swiss policy and market context anchors the analysis in real institutions. AHV 21 shows that retirement parameters are being adjusted inside an environment of longer old-age survival (Federal Social Insurance Office, 2025). The 13th AHV pension introduces a benefit-design issue whose fiscal weight depends partly on how many retirees collect and for how long (Federal Social Insurance Office, 2026). On the private side, FINMA's statistics describe a life sector with substantial technical liabilities, a heavy occupational-pension component, and a concentrated group of major insurers (FINMA, 2025). These institutions live on credible mortality assumptions, because small shifts in old-age survival can move large portfolios of long-duration obligations.

The APVB result is where the two halves of the report meet. A whole life death benefit and a pension annuity respond in opposite directions to the same mortality improvement. For whole life, delayed death lowers the present value of the fixed benefit under positive discounting. For pensions and annuities, delayed death lengthens the payment stream and raises exposure to systematic longevity risk. That contrast is the central operational finding of the report, and it links the empirical life-table work to the practical actuarial valuation problem.

## 11. Conclusion

Across ten HMD benchmark years from 1988 to 2024, Swiss period life tables show sustained mortality improvement. Life expectancy measures rise. The Lexis Point moves toward older ages. Survival curves hold higher for longer. Deaths concentrate at advanced ages. These patterns line up with both mortality compression and rectangularization of the survival curve, with the caveat that the evidence is built on selected snapshots and visual life-table reading rather than formal statistical testing.

The gender story carries both persistence and convergence. Females retain higher life expectancy and lower APVB on the whole life death benefit, which reflects higher survival and later expected death. Males start from a lower longevity position but improve more rapidly, producing partial catch-up and closer modal-age alignment. So sex-specific mortality work stays in the toolkit, but historical gender gaps cannot be treated as fixed parameters.

The actuarial implications track product design rather than demography in isolation. For AHV/AVS, BVG/LPP, and annuity-style liabilities, longer survival lengthens expected payment duration and strengthens systematic longevity risk. For the simplified whole life contract, mortality improvement tends to lower APVB because the fixed death benefit is expected later and is more heavily discounted. The 2020 disruption followed by 2024 recovery reinforces the practical need to distinguish short-run period shocks from the longer-run improvement trend.

The report also leaves total life expectancy in its proper context. The WHO TLE-HALE comparison shows that high Swiss longevity coexists with a material gap between total years lived and years expected in full health (World Health Organization, n.d.). That gap matters for long-term care, health insurance, pension adequacy, retirement-age policy, and household financial planning. A longer life is not automatically a longer healthy working life, and the actuarial reading should not blur the two.

A handful of limitations bound everything above. The analysis uses period rather than cohort life tables, so it describes mortality conditions in selected years rather than the lifetime experience of real birth cohorts. The benchmark-year design is good for historical comparison but does not use the full annual HMD series. The Gompertz and Gompertz-Makeham material in Section 5 is exploratory — no parameter estimates, standard errors, residual diagnostics, or goodness-of-fit tests are reported. The APVB calculation excludes expenses, lapses, underwriting classes, taxes, currency effects, stochastic interest rates, and capital costs. The reported APVB percentage decline still needs to be checked against the underlying spreadsheet before submission: [CHECK APVB RESULT TABLE]. WHO health-expectancy data sit in a different source framework and should not be mechanically merged with HMD curtate life expectancy.

A natural next step would be to swap the period basis for cohort life tables, which would let us compare period and cohort interpretations of Swiss longevity head-to-head and quantify the cohort effect that period tables suppress by construction. Beyond that, a stochastic mortality model — Lee-Carter, Cairns-Blake-Dowd, or a more recent variant — would let us put confidence intervals around future longevity risk rather than leaving the forecast implicit. The compression and rectangularization claims also deserve formal measurement: dispersion around the modal age at death, defined rectangularization metrics, and tests of whether the observed shifts are statistically distinguishable from prior years. On the product side, the APVB exercise extends naturally into immediate and deferred annuity pricing, which would put the opposing financial signs of mortality improvement on the same chart. And on the morbidity side, linking healthy life expectancy to long-term care insurance valuation and disability-related cost projections would tie this report's longevity work to the morbidity and care-financing questions that sit one step beyond it.

## References

Federal Social Insurance Office. (2025). *AHV 21*. Swiss Confederation. https://www.bsv.admin.ch/de/ahv-21

Federal Social Insurance Office. (2026). *Umsetzung der Initiative fur eine 13. AHV-Rente*. Swiss Confederation. https://www.bsv.admin.ch/de/umsetzung-13-ahv-rente

FINMA. (2025). *Insurance market report 2024*. Swiss Financial Market Supervisory Authority. https://www.finma.ch/en/~/media/finma/dokumente/dokumentencenter/myfinma/finma-publikationen/versicherungsbericht/20250903-versicherungsmarktbericht-2024.pdf

Human Mortality Database. (2026). *Switzerland period life tables*. University of California, Berkeley, and Max Planck Institute for Demographic Research. Retrieved May 25, 2026, from https://www.mortality.org/

Pitacco, E., & Carmeci, G. (2019). *Longevity trends: Historical aspects and recent issues*. DemoLab Paper 01/2019. MIB Trieste School of Management.

Spedicato, G. ([CHECK REFERENCE DETAILS]). *Life contingencies*. In *Computational Actuarial Science with R*.

Swiss Confederation. (n.d.-a). *The 2nd pillar: Occupational pension*. ch.ch. Retrieved May 25, 2026, from https://www.ch.ch/en/retirement/old-age-pension/the-2nd-pillar/

Swiss Confederation. (n.d.-b). *The 3rd pillar: Private pension provision*. ch.ch. Retrieved May 25, 2026, from https://www.ch.ch/en/retirement/old-age-pension/the-3rd-pillar/

Valdez, E. A. (2019). *Life tables and selection: Lecture weeks 4-5*. Math 3630. [CHECK REFERENCE DETAILS]

World Health Organization. (n.d.). *Switzerland: Health data overview for the Swiss Confederation*. WHO Data. Retrieved May 25, 2026, from https://data.who.int/countries/756
